justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Scenario A correctly identifies no circuit breaker causing sustained 2s timeout degradation with specific fix (add circuit breaker, fallback policy). (2) Scenario B includes connection pool math (20 workers / 10 connections = contention) and predicts failure modes. (3) Scenario C explicitly identifies that slow-but-successful calls won't trip the circuit breaker as a critical resilience gap — called out as 'the most dangerous scenario'. (4) Scenario D correctly assesses circuit breaker as effective, noting it trips after 5 failures and provides fast-fail thereafter. (5) Specific improvements provided for each failing scenario. (6) Verdict is NOT READY, correctly identifying A, B, C as unhandled.

observations.text: Scenario C analysis is exceptional — correctly identifies circuit breaker blindness to latency, calculates throughput (20 threads / 8s = 2 req/s), and predicts unbounded queue growth leading to OOM
Scenario B goes beyond the rubric by identifying the ghost charge data integrity risk (Stripe charge succeeds but DB write fails)
Scenario D correctly differentiated as the best-handled case with partial rating, while still identifying the 50s slow-failure window before circuit breaker trips
Scenario A thread starvation math is precise: 20 threads ÷ 2s timeout = max 10 req/s throughput, all failing
Observability review adds significant value beyond what was required — identifies specific missing metrics needed to distinguish between the four failure modes
Confidence section transparently lists assumptions and low-confidence areas, particularly around arrival rate and DB pool blocking behavior
Minor: Scenario A states 'functionally dead' which slightly overstates — the rubric notes 'service survives but is degraded', though the output does also say 'Service stays up'
